channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: To Get the Strait Open, Trump Had to Leave the Hardest Issues for Later
item.description: President Trump is hailing the agreement with Iran as groundbreaking, even as he admits it “isn’t even fully negotiated.” But the nuclear stockpile, enrichment and missiles have not been discussed.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger and Tyler Pager
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Arms Control and Limitation and Disarmament
item.media:description: The Strait of Hormuz has been a focal point since the start of the conflict.

item.title: Reopening of Strait of Hormuz Still a Question Mark
item.description: Without the details of a formal agreement, it is unclear how soon normal shipping will resume and when oil prices will start to come down.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: About 1,500 to 2,000 ships have been trapped in the Persian Gulf by the conflict.

item.title: Senate Republicans Cast Doubt on a Potential Peace Deal With Iran
item.description: Lawmakers, including some of President Trump’s closest allies, slammed the emerging agreement as effectively undermining the president’s own war goals.
item.dc:creator: Minho Kim
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Senator Thom Tillis said it was “questionable” that Iran would end its blockade on the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: What to Know About the Citizenship Lists Trump Wants to Create
item.description: President Trump is trying to create individual lists of citizens by state to determine who can vote, even as his administration acknowledges they would be unreliable.
item.dc:creator: Adam Sella
item.media:description: President Trump signed an executive order in March that called on states to use federal databases to identify noncitizens.

item.title: Confusion and Worry After Trump Administration’s Abrupt Green Card Changes
item.description: Immigrants and their advocates and lawyers are trying to interpret a new Trump administration rule that requires people to be in their native country to apply for a green card.
item.dc:creator: Christina Morales
item.media:description: The Citizenship and Immigration Services agency oversees the citizenship system.

item.title: Caught Flat-Footed, a City Races to Catch Up With Ebola
item.description: The deadly virus has spread alarmingly in Congo for months. Only now is the response taking shape.
item.dc:creator: Declan Walsh and Arlette Bashizi
item.media:description: Disinfecting the public market on Saturday in Bunia, in the Democratic Republic of Congo’s northeast, as part of the response to the Ebola virus’s resurgence.

item.title: What to Know About the Ebola Outbreak as Cases and Deaths Rise
item.description: More than 170 deaths and about 750 cases are suspected. The United States restricted entry for people who have been to three African countries.
item.dc:creator: Ephrat Livni and Lynsey Chutel
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A hand-washing station in Goma, Democratic Republic of Congo, on Thursday.

item.title: $140,000 E.V.s and Heritage Gold: The Rise of China’s Homegrown Luxury Market
item.description: As China’s economy slows, consumers are increasingly turning to domestic luxury products, challenging the dominance of long-established European brands.
item.dc:creator: Joy Dong
item.media:credit: Gilles Sabrié for The New York Times
item.media:description: Li Maozai in his Maextro S800 sedan, built by Huawei and JAC Motors.

item.title: They Started I.V.F., Then Split. Now Who Gets Custody of the Embryos?
item.description: For 47-year-old Erin Millender, this will likely be her last chance to become a mother. Her husband no longer wants to have a child with her.
item.dc:creator: Caroline Kitchener
item.media:credit: Kim Raff for The New York Times

item.title: Pope Leo Is Set to Release an Encyclical About A.I. Why Is That Important?
item.description: Leo XIV is to issue his thoughts about artificial intelligence in the modern world, using a centuries-old form of papal communication called an encyclical, the first since he became pope.
item.dc:creator: Elisabetta Povoledo
item.media:description: Pope Leo has made artificial intelligence a central theme of his first year as pontiff.

item.title: UK Institute Is Hunting for Dangers Lurking in AI
item.description: The government’s A.I. Security Institute, staffed by alumni from OpenAI and Google, is becoming a model for countries grappling with A.I.’s emerging risks.
item.dc:creator: Adam Satariano and Paul Mozur
item.media:credit: Jeremie Souteyrat for The New York Times
item.media:description: Along Parliament Square in London is the home of the A.I. Security Institute, which employs weapons inspectors, epidemiologists and code breakers to probe the technology’s risks.

item.title: Bystander Wounded in White House Shooting Is in Stable Condition
item.description: The Metropolitan Police Department is investigating to determine who shot the bystander, who underwent surgery, and how many bullets were fired.
item.dc:creator: Katie Benner
item.media:credit: Allison Robbert for The New York Times
item.media:description: Law enforcement officers near the White House after a shooting on Saturday evening.

item.title: What to Know About the White House Shooting on Memorial Day Weekend
item.description: Shots were fired near the northwestern corner of the White House complex, while President Trump was inside the building. The Secret Service returned fire, killing the gunman.
item.dc:creator: Campbell Robertson and Chelsia Rose Marcius
item.media:description: Shots were fired at the northwestern corner of the White House grounds.

item.title: Trump Endorsement Shakes John Cornyn in Texas Senate Runoff Against Ken Paxton
item.description: A tough re-election race grew more daunting after President Trump backed Mr. Cornyn’s opponent, Ken Paxton. The Texas senator has vowed to fight to the end.
item.dc:creator: J. David Goodman
item.media:credit: Jordan Vonderhaar for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator John Cornyn of Texas, left, greeted voters at a campaign event in San Antonio ahead of his primary election runoff.

item.title: A Democrat Took on Red Sox Ownership in an Ad. A Network Pulled it.
item.description: Graham Platner, a Senate candidate in Maine, blamed private equity for destroying “our favorite baseball team.” NESN, which is owned by the team, pulled his campaign ad, citing an intellectual property violation.
item.dc:creator: Taylor Robinson and Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Graham Platner, a candidate for Senate in Maine, speaking at a town hall in Sabattus, Maine, last month.

item.title: In Homeless Crisis, California ‘Is Waging a War on R.V.s’
item.description: The R.V.s are seen as an eyesore — the most visible sign of the state’s homelessness crisis. Neighbors and politicians want them gone. The people who call them home feel under siege.
item.dc:creator: Bradley Berman
item.media:description: Los Angeles in early January. A new California law allows certain authorities to quickly demolish oversize vehicles worth up to $4,000, up from $500, if the owner cannot move the vehicle without help from a tow truck.

item.title: A Homeowner Built Townhouses in a Jersey City Backyard
item.description: As YIMBYs and NIMBYs debate where to build housing, a project in Jersey City, N.J. shows how seemingly irreconcilable sides can find common ground.
item.dc:creator: Jane Margolies

item.title: As Rents Soar, Londoners Turn to Vacant Pubs, Offices and Police Stations
item.description: Property guardianship, in which residents live in otherwise vacant buildings, used to be considered edgy. But as the rental market tightens, more people are embracing the arrangement.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Wolfe and Alice Zoo For The New York Times
item.media:credit: Alice Zoo for The New York Times
item.media:description: Once a boutique hotel, The Kings Arms Hotel in London is now a guardianship property.

item.title: How Wine, Truffles and Honey Could Help Europe Fight Wildfires
item.description: Countries are learning that the finer things in life sometimes have a serendipitous side benefit.
item.dc:creator: Jeanna Smialek
item.media:credit: Samuel Aranda for The New York Times

item.title: Crack Forms in Garden Grove Chemical Tank, a Positive Sign for Firefighters
item.description: The tank in Southern California has been building in temperature and pressure for days, which could lead to an explosive release. The crack may reduce some of that pressure, officials said.
item.dc:creator: Charlotte Dulany and Shawn Hubler
item.media:description: An industrial tank in Southern California is at risk of rupture or explosion that could send toxic chemicals into the surrounding area.

item.title: For Carlo Petrini, the Point of ‘Slow Food’ Wasn’t the Food. It Was Us.
item.description: The Italian activist, who died on Thursday, built his global movement on the idea that eating well could make modern life more meaningful.
item.dc:creator: Pete Wells

item.title: Carlo Petrini, Whose Slow Food Movement Transformed How We Eat, Dies at 76
item.description: Through his Slow Food organization, he spurred a worldwide shift toward sustainability and thoughtful cuisine.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.media:description: Carlo Petrini in 2012. He urged people at all points in the supply chain to embrace food that he described as “good, clean and fair.”

item.title: The Ghosts of France’s Slave Trade Will Be Silenced No More
item.description: Three centuries late and not a moment too soon.
item.dc:creator: Andrew S. Curran
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations

item.title: My Wife Fought Corruption in El Salvador. Then She Vanished Into Its Prisons.
item.description: The cost of Nayib Bukele’s crackdown on crime is too high.
item.dc:creator: Louis Benavides
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations

item.title: In Ukraine, a Divisive 20th-Century Hero Comes Home
item.description: Andriy Melnyk, criticized as a Nazi collaborator and lionized as an anti-Soviet resistance leader, was given state honors for his reburial near Kyiv.
item.dc:creator: Andrew E. Kramer
item.media:credit: Andriy Zhyhaylo/Global Images Ukraine, via Getty Images
item.media:description: The coffins of Andriy Melnyk and his wife, Sofia Fedak-Melnyk, in Kyiv, Ukraine, on Friday.

item.title: Israel’s President, Isaac Herzog, Denounces Israeli Violence
item.description: In a speech, Isaac Herzog, whose role is largely ceremonial, delivered a grave indictment of settler attacks in the West Bank and abuse of prisoners.
item.dc:creator: Isabel Kershner
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:description: President Isaac Herzog of Israel in Panama this month.

item.title: Manny Nosowsky, Waggish Crossword Constructor for The New York Times, Dies at 94
item.description: For nearly two decades, the prolific puzzle maker, a retired urologist, charmed solvers with his penchant for witty, waggish puns.
item.dc:creator: Deb Amlen
item.media:credit: Photo illustration by The New York Times. Photo, via Megalo Media, Inc.
item.media:description: Manny Nosowsky holds the record for the most puzzles published in The Times.
